note: the design doc's pivot family-tree case. memberCount=96 is exactly MAX_FAMILY_MEMBERS — the dynasty overflows the cap and reports truncated=true rather than silently dropping kin. mustInclude covers both the near-root breadth (Aenys/Maegor, gen 1, direct sons) and the deep main-line spine (Daenerys/Rhaegar/Aerys II/Aegon V at gen 9-13, and the Blackfyre cadet split) — the spine exists specifically to reach descendants 12 hops past the default generationsDown=4 breadth horizon. generationCounts are prominence-sensitive (degree + 4*quoteCount picks who makes the cap), so enrichment passes legitimately reshuffle the per-generation split — repinned S203 after the F&B deferred-events mint raised Dance-era principals' degree (gen 5: 3->8). mustInclude is the real invariant; verify it still passes before repinning counts.

an explicit tight window (generationsDown below the default of 4) opts OUT of the deep main-line spine entirely — verified live. All members stay within the requested [-1, 1] generation bound; the deep spine only threads when generationsDown >= the default.

a clean (non-era-collided) lineage, small enough to assert the full member set. Catelyn is attached as a spouse at generation 0 (root's own generation) rather than via a PARENT_OF walk. Live snapshot 2026-07-04: 17 members, not truncated.
restates graph_test.ts's spouse-attachment + generation-bound cases as a shared golden case
the ONE case that returns the fully-empty FamilyTreeResult: a slug the validate.ts regex trust boundary rejects outright (contains '..' / '/'). Restates graph_test.ts's invalid-slug case.

NOT previously documented anywhere: familyTree on a well-formed but non-existent slug (passes the regex trust boundary, but no node record and no edges reference it) returns a ONE-MEMBER tree containing a synthetic stub for the queried slug itself -- NOT an empty tree. This differs from resolve() ([] on any miss) and neighbors()/walkChain() (empty groups/arrays on invalid input). See operations.md's family section for the full explanation.
